provenance.review_method: Model-assisted structural review of every supplied evidence note; no systematic full-text verification of the cited corpus

evidence.title: Pandemic emerged in Wuhan, home to the world's leading bat-SARS-coronavirus lab, ~1,500-1,800 km from the nearest known viral reservoir
evidence.claim: A novel SARS-related coronavirus first erupted in Wuhan, the city hosting the Wuhan Institute of Virology (WIV) — the world's leading collector and laboratory manipulator of SARS-like bat coronaviruses — even though the closest known wild relatives circulate in horseshoe bats in Yunnan (China) and Laos, roughly 1,500-1,800 km away from Wuhan. Lab-leak proponents argue this geographic juxtaposition is an extraordinary coincidence.
evidence.evidence_quality:
The component facts are strong and primary-sourced: WIV's collection (peer-reviewed virology literature, institutional records), RaTG13's Mojiang provenance and distance (Zhou et al. 2020 Nature; reported distances), and BANAL-52's Laos provenance (Temmam et al. 2022, Nature, peer-reviewed). So the *factual substrate* — virus in Wuhan, top lab in Wuhan, reservoir ~1,500-1,800 km away — is robust (confidence high).

However, the *inferential weight* is circumstantial. The claim describes a spatial coincidence; it contains no genomic, documentary, or epidemiological evidence that the lab was the actual source. It is the kind of evidence that constrains priors rather than identifies a mechanism.
evidence.strongest_counterargument:
The proximity is real but weakly diagnostic, for several reasons (Wikipedia "COVID-19 lab leak theory"; Lewandowsky et al.):
- **Labs cluster where people cluster.** Most large Chinese cities host coronavirus research laboratories; Wuhan (~11 million people) is a major transport, trade, and wildlife-trade hub. Outbreaks of bat-origin viruses "typically begin in rural areas but are first noticed in large cities," so any spillover plausibly surfaces in a big surveilled city regardless of labs.
- **Conjunction fallacy.** Lewandowsky and colleagues argue treating the lab's location as *a priori* evidence is "literally a coincidence" reasoning — lab-leak proponents foreground the WIV while ignoring the equally-present wet markets known to be spillover venues.
- **Positive natural-origin evidence at the same site.** Spatial-epidemiology analyses (Worobey et al. 2022, Science; Pekar et al. 2022, Science) place the earliest cases and environmental positives at the Huanan Seafood Market, where live wild mammals susceptible to SARS-CoV-2 were sold — a competing, mechanism-bearing explanation for *why Wuhan*. If the market is the epicenter, the WIV's presence becomes incidental.
- WIV's main campus is ~12 km from the Huanan market; the within-city co-location is not itself unusual for a metropolis.

In short: proximity establishes that a leak was *possible*, but a wildlife-trade spillover in a major hub city is at least as consistent with the same geography.
evidence.confidence_assessment:
- **Claim confidence:** 0.9 — The underlying facts (novel SARS-CoV in Wuhan; WIV as the top bat-CoV lab there; nearest known relatives ~1,500-1,800 km away in Yunnan/Laos) are well-sourced and largely uncontested. The minor uncertainty is wording: "1,500-1,900 km" is approximate, the true ancestor/reservoir location is unknown, and "closest relatives" is a moving target (RaTG13 → BANAL-52).
- **Support strength:** weak — for the lab-leak hypothesis. As pure circumstance it raises the lab-leak prior modestly but is symmetrically explainable by natural spillover in a dense trade/travel hub, and is undercut by positive market-centered epidemiological evidence. It is suggestive, not dispositive.
- **Net:** A calibrated reasoner should accept the geographic coincidence as true and genuinely notable, while recognizing it is opportunity-only evidence that moves the needle only slightly absent a mechanism — and that the same city-level geography is independently predicted by the wildlife-trade hypothesis.

framing_assumptions: Wuhan is selected after the event as a city unusually associated with bat-coronavirus research, making the co-location appear diagnostically rare.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on the reference class of cities, labs, outbreak-detection hubs, wildlife trade routes, and whether location hypotheses were specified in advance.
coverage_gaps: No quantified comparison estimates the probability of Wuhan under laboratory and natural-trade pathways using symmetric geographic models.
correlation_risks: Correlated with WIV capability and biosafety evidence, while competing market geography is represented by several overlapping items.
capability_limits: Circumstantial patterns are especially sensitive to base rates, alternative explanations, and selection of which coincidences are noticed.

evidential: The co-location is real; its likelihood ratio is not established.
framing_conditional: Choosing the lab, market, city, or regional trade network as the salient geographic unit changes the apparent coincidence.

notes: A clear example of reference-class and salience choices entering before assessment.

evidence.title: Egg production has environmental impacts that vary by system and comparison
evidence.claim: An environmental reading of “good to eat” evaluates greenhouse-gas emissions, land use, feed production, manure, eutrophication, and other impacts; results depend on production system, functional unit, geography, and the food used as a comparator.
evidence.evidence_quality: Life-cycle assessment is the appropriate general method, but estimates are model-dependent. System boundaries, allocation choices, feed assumptions, geography, and the unit of comparison can materially change results.
evidence.strongest_counterargument: Comparative global averages can obscure local production practices and nutritional substitution. A per-kilogram, per-protein, per-calorie, and per-serving comparison can rank foods differently, so an environmental verdict without a declared functional unit is underspecified.
evidence.confidence_assessment: No numeric confidence is assigned. The robust conclusion is structural: environmental assessment requires a different source universe and outcome definition than either health or welfare assessment.

evidence.title: Individual lipid responses to dietary cholesterol from eggs vary
evidence.claim: Individuals differ in their serum-lipid response to dietary cholesterol from eggs, so a population-average association does not fully answer whether a particular person will experience a clinically relevant change.
evidence.evidence_quality: Controlled feeding studies support the existence of heterogeneous biomarker responses, but many are small, short, and use egg doses above ordinary consumption. Definitions of a “hyper-responder” vary, and long-term clinical relevance remains less certain than the short-term lipid response.
evidence.strongest_counterargument: The heterogeneity may have limited clinical importance if LDL and HDL change together, if effects remain within healthy ranges, or if the observed response does not translate into different cardiovascular outcomes. Small studies can also exaggerate apparent responder subgroups.
evidence.confidence_assessment: No numeric confidence is assigned. The evidence supports splitting a single population-level claim into a population warrant and a person-specific uncertainty statement; it does not support diagnosing an individual from the literature alone.

evidence.title: Egg production creates husbandry-dependent laying-hen welfare harms
evidence.claim: Whether eggs are “good to eat” can reasonably include the welfare of laying hens; welfare outcomes depend on housing and management and include risks such as bone lesions, restriction of behavior, group stress, and other husbandry-related harms.
evidence.evidence_quality: The EFSA opinion synthesizes peer-reviewed and grey literature, expert elicitation, and uncertainty analysis. It is authoritative for the questions it addresses, while also reporting limited evidence for some populations and uncertain relationships among group size, stocking density, and stress.
evidence.strongest_counterargument: Animal-welfare evidence does not itself determine whether eating eggs is morally acceptable. That conclusion also depends on ethical commitments, production system, geography, substitution effects, and how competing welfare outcomes are weighted.
evidence.confidence_assessment: No numeric confidence is assigned. The key epistemic point is that a population-health framing excludes this evidence by construction, not because the welfare considerations were assessed and found wanting.

evidence.title: Moderate egg consumption and population-level cardiovascular risk
evidence.claim: At a population level, moderate egg consumption—roughly up to one egg per day—has not shown a consistent association with higher cardiovascular-disease risk across prospective-cohort meta-analyses, although results vary by population and study design.
evidence.evidence_quality: The BMJ paper includes large prospective cohorts and a systematic review, but the evidence is observational and vulnerable to residual confounding, dietary-measurement error, and variation in what foods eggs replace or accompany. The AHA advisory is an expert synthesis rather than an independent experimental result.
evidence.strongest_counterargument: Other large observational analyses have reported positive associations between dietary cholesterol or egg intake and cardiovascular outcomes. Aggregating population averages may also conceal meaningful individual response differences and subgroup effects.
evidence.confidence_assessment: No numeric confidence is assigned in this starter baseline. The warranted conclusion is conditional: moderate intake is not consistently associated with higher population-level cardiovascular risk, but this does not establish individual safety or settle questions about other health outcomes.

policies.question_raw: Was COVID-19 caused by zoonotic spillover or a research-related incident?

framing.operationalized_question: Which proximate-origin hypothesis is better supported by the accessible evidence?

exclusion_rules: Material not reliably interpretable in English

capability_profile.note: The imported baseline did not publish a measured language-capability profile.

framing.operationalized_question: What welfare consequences for laying hens are causally connected to producing the eggs being consumed?

inclusion_rules: Evidence tied to laying-hen production systems and welfare outcomes
exclusion_rules: Human-health outcomes alone cannot answer this framing

capability_profile.capability: Separate descriptive welfare evidence from ethical weighting
capability_profile.note: The evidence can describe consequences; the moral conclusion requires declared values.

framing.operationalized_question: What environmental impacts follow from egg production relative to plausible substitute foods?

inclusion_rules: Studies must state system boundaries and a functional unit
exclusion_rules: Health and welfare outcomes are tracked separately

capability_profile.note: Rankings depend on functional unit, geography, and substitution assumptions.

framing.operationalized_question: How might eating eggs affect an individual's lipid markers and cardiovascular risk, given heterogeneous dietary-cholesterol response?

inclusion_rules: Evidence must report or discuss variation between individuals
exclusion_rules: Population averages are insufficient when response heterogeneity is not reported

capability_profile.capability: Infer an individual's response from population literature
capability_profile.note: The tool can surface heterogeneity but cannot diagnose a user.

framing.operationalized_question: At typical consumption levels, how is eating eggs associated with population-level cardiovascular outcomes?

inclusion_rules: Human evidence with an explicit egg-consumption exposure
exclusion_rules: Animal welfare and environmental outcomes are outside this framing

capability_profile.note: Full methodological appraisal still requires domain review.

experiments.question: Was COVID-19 caused by zoonotic spillover or a research-related incident?

assessment_method.aggregation: Within each independence cluster, retain only the maximum upstream support strength for each direction; then sum cluster contributions.
assessment_method.interpretation: The resulting balance is an exploratory sensitivity measure based on the baseline's support labels, not a calibrated probability or an independent truth judgment.

known_but_unavailable.label: Independent early-case line list
known_but_unavailable.note: The baseline repeatedly identifies raw early-case locations, exposure histories, and case-finding procedures as unavailable for independent audit.

known_but_unavailable.label: Underlying intelligence
known_but_unavailable.note: Public agency conclusions do not expose the classified sources, dependency structure, or analytic tradecraft behind them.

known_but_unavailable.label: Laboratory inventories and incident records
known_but_unavailable.note: Complete WIV sequence inventories, laboratory notebooks, incident logs, and staff medical records are not available in the supplied corpus.

known_but_unavailable.label: Contemporaneous animal and supply-chain samples
known_but_unavailable.note: The relevant live animals were removed before systematic sampling, and upstream suppliers were not comprehensively investigated in the available record.

capability_limited.label: Sequence-level genomic adjudication
capability_limited.note: The annotation pass did not independently reproduce phylogenetic, molecular-clock, codon, or recombination analyses.

capability_limited.label: Spatial and ascertainment modeling
capability_limited.note: The annotation pass did not reproduce competing spatial models from raw case data.

capability_limited.label: Non-English full-text interpretation
capability_limited.note: The imported baseline does not provide a measured language-coverage or language-reliability profile.

not_established.label: Systematic non-English source coverage
not_established.note: This prototype cannot yet prove which relevant non-English sources were excluded; it records the missing coverage measurement rather than inventing a source list.

not_established.label: Complete source universe
not_established.note: The 173 normalized citations are the sources in the supplied baseline, not an enumeration of all relevant evidence that exists.

clusters.label: Market and early cases
clusters.description: Early-case geography, market linkage, ascertainment, and the composite market-origin argument.

clusters.label: Natural relatives and mechanisms
clusters.description: Natural sarbecovirus diversity, recombination, receptor binding, and distance from known relatives.

clusters.label: Furin-site features
clusters.description: Sequence-feature arguments about the furin-cleavage site and codon pair.

clusters.label: Opacity and data access
clusters.description: Withholding, deletion, disclosure disputes, publication process, and institutional reversals.

clusters.label: DEFUSE proposal
clusters.description: Three records describing the same 2018 proposal and its relationship to later viral features.

clusters.label: Market animals and samples
clusters.description: Live susceptible mammals, environmental samples, wildlife DNA, and the failure to identify an infected animal.

clusters.label: Intelligence and official assessments
clusters.description: Public summaries of intelligence judgments, congressional synthesis, and reported staff illness.

clusters.label: Laboratory capability and biosafety
clusters.description: Relevant experimental capability, containment practice, staffing concerns, and serial-passage possibility.

clusters.label: Mojiang and RaTG13 provenance
clusters.description: Mine illness, sample history, naming, and delayed disclosure around RaTG13.

clusters.label: Lineages and timing
clusters.description: Molecular-clock dating and duplicated arguments for multiple early introductions.

clusters.label: Missing progenitor or host
clusters.description: The unresolved absence of a close viral progenitor or identified intermediate host.

clusters.label: Zoonotic precedents
clusters.description: SARS-CoV-1 and MERS as reference classes for coronavirus emergence.

clusters.label: Wuhan geographic coincidence
clusters.description: The geographic co-location of outbreak recognition and a leading bat-coronavirus laboratory.

runs.label: Strict public evidence
runs.description: Retains public empirical and mechanistic evidence. It excludes intelligence judgments, institutional conduct, general biosafety context, and circumstantial coincidence as independent origin evidence.

assessment.conclusion: The retained annotated support favors natural origin.
assessment.method_note: The resulting balance is an exploratory sensitivity measure based on the baseline's support labels, not a calibrated probability or an independent truth judgment.

runs.label: Contextual evidence
runs.description: Adds documentary records, biosafety practice, and published intelligence assessments while keeping purely circumstantial coincidence outside the assessment.

assessment.conclusion: The retained annotated support is mixed between the two hypotheses.
assessment.method_note: The resulting balance is an exploratory sensitivity measure based on the baseline's support labels, not a calibrated probability or an independent truth judgment.

runs.label: Full available baseline
runs.description: Retains all 40 evidence notes in the supplied Carlo/Claude corpus. This is the full available baseline, not a claim to contain all evidence that exists.

assessment.conclusion: The retained annotated support is mixed between the two hypotheses.
assessment.method_note: The resulting balance is an exploratory sensitivity measure based on the baseline's support labels, not a calibrated probability or an independent truth judgment.

Market and early cases
nodes.description: Early-case geography, market linkage, ascertainment, and the composite market-origin argument.

nodes.label: Natural relatives and mechanisms
nodes.description: Natural sarbecovirus diversity, recombination, receptor binding, and distance from known relatives.

nodes.label: Furin-site features
nodes.description: Sequence-feature arguments about the furin-cleavage site and codon pair.

nodes.label: Opacity and data access
nodes.description: Withholding, deletion, disclosure disputes, publication process, and institutional reversals.

nodes.label: DEFUSE proposal
nodes.description: Three records describing the same 2018 proposal and its relationship to later viral features.

nodes.label: Market animals and samples
nodes.description: Live susceptible mammals, environmental samples, wildlife DNA, and the failure to identify an infected animal.

nodes.label: Intelligence and official assessments
nodes.description: Public summaries of intelligence judgments, congressional synthesis, and reported staff illness.

nodes.label: Laboratory capability and biosafety
nodes.description: Relevant experimental capability, containment practice, staffing concerns, and serial-passage possibility.

nodes.label: Mojiang and RaTG13 provenance
nodes.description: Mine illness, sample history, naming, and delayed disclosure around RaTG13.

nodes.label: Lineages and timing
nodes.description: Molecular-clock dating and duplicated arguments for multiple early introductions.

nodes.label: Missing progenitor or host
nodes.description: The unresolved absence of a close viral progenitor or identified intermediate host.

nodes.label: Zoonotic precedents
nodes.description: SARS-CoV-1 and MERS as reference classes for coronavirus emergence.

nodes.label: Wuhan geographic coincidence
nodes.description: The geographic co-location of outbreak recognition and a leading bat-coronavirus laboratory.

nodes.label: 2018 U.S. State Department cables flagged a trained-staff shortage at WIV's new BSL-4 lab
nodes.claim: In January 2018, U.S. Embassy Beijing science diplomats who visited the Wuhan Institute of Virology (WIV) sent at least one "Sensitive But Unclassified" cable to Washington noting that the WIV's newly opened BSL-4 laboratory faced "a serious shortage of appropriately trained technicians and investigators needed to safely operate this high-containment laboratory," in the context of WIV research on bat coronaviruses with human-transmission potential. The cables requested more U.S. assistance; they did not allege an actual leak.
coverage_gaps: The complete cable context, facility-specific incident records, and the containment level of the hypothesized precursor work are not established by this item.

nodes.label: A staffing concern at a high-containment facility is treated as relevant to the probability of a specific accident involving a possibly different laboratory workflow.
policy_sensitivities: Weight changes sharply depending on whether the policy admits retrospective circumstantial indicators or requires incident-specific evidence.
correlation_risks: Overlaps with the BSL-2 handling and general WIV biosafety items; these should not be counted as three independent accident signals.

nodes.label: Both founding lineages tie to the Huanan market, and ascertainment-bias checks suggest the spatial clustering is not an artifact
nodes.claim:
Both early SARS-CoV-2 lineages (A and B) are linked to the Huanan Seafood Wholesale Market — lineage B in human cases directly associated with the market, lineage A in a market environmental sample and in early human cases who lived near the market — and dedicated analyses argue that the tight spatial clustering of the earliest December 2019 cases around the market is not an artifact of how cases were found.

> **Important correction to the working summary.** The original framing ("Both lineage A and lineage B were detected in samples directly associated with the market") is only partly correct and must be stated carefully. Lineage B is the one found in the eleven sequenced human genomes *directly associated* with the market. Lineage A was **not** found in those direct human market cases; it was detected in a market **environmental** sample (collected after market closure) and in early human cases who *lived or had recently stayed near* the market. The "both lineages at the market" claim is defensible only if "environmental sample + near-market cases" counts, not if it means "both in human cases directly linked to the market."
coverage_gaps: Independent raw line-list data and a complete account of early case-finding procedures are unavailable.

nodes.label: The observed early-case set is sufficiently representative for absence of a second cluster to be informative.
policy_sensitivities: Assessment changes with case-definition rules, treatment of market environmental samples, and how ascertainment-bias critiques are weighted.
correlation_risks: Substantially overlaps the market-spatial-clustering and two-lineage evidence cluster.

nodes.label: BANAL bat sarbecoviruses in Laos: closest known SARS-CoV-2 relatives, with RBDs that bind human ACE2 and infect human cells in nature
nodes.claim: Wild horseshoe bats (*Rhinolophus* spp.) in karst caves of Feuang district, northern Laos, harbor sarbecoviruses (BANAL-52, BANAL-103, BANAL-236) whose genomes are up to ~96.8% identical to SARS-CoV-2 — the closest known relatives at the time of publication. Their receptor-binding domains (RBDs) are nearly identical to SARS-CoV-2's, bind human ACE2 with affinity comparable to or higher than early SARS-CoV-2, and the viruses enter and replicate in human cells via ACE2 without prior adaptation. This rebuts the early "no wild virus binds human ACE2 this well, so the RBD must be engineered" argument.
coverage_gaps: Known BANAL viruses do not bridge the complete genomic gap or explain every SARS-CoV-2 feature, including the furin-cleavage-site question.

nodes.label: Natural availability of ACE2-binding relatives materially addresses origin hypotheses centered on an allegedly unnatural receptor-binding domain.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on whether the target hypothesis is rational RBD design, any engineering, serial passage, or research-related collection of a natural virus.
correlation_risks: Shares mechanism-level support with natural-recombination, pangolin-RBD, and genetic-distance items.

nodes.label: The CGG-CGG arginine codon pair at the furin cleavage site is a weak, non-probative origin clue
nodes.claim: The two arginines of the SARS-CoV-2 spike furin cleavage motif (P-R-R-A-R) are encoded by the codon doublet CGG-CGG. CGG is the rarest of the six arginine codons in SARS-CoV-2 (roughly 3–5% of arginine codons), and the consecutive CGG-CGG doublet is uncommon in sarbecoviruses. Lab-leak proponents argued this is the codon a molecular biologist would preferentially choose, hinting at human design.
coverage_gaps: A preregistered likelihood model for the doublet under competing mechanisms is absent.

nodes.label: A rare codon pair is assumed to be more expected under deliberate construction than under natural evolution.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on the reference class used for codon rarity and on whether designer preferences are specified before observing the sequence.
correlation_risks: This is one feature of the broader furin-cleavage-site cluster and should not be counted independently from it.

nodes.label: China withheld and delayed key origin data, obstructing the investigation
nodes.claim: China repeatedly delayed, restricted, or did not release primary data needed to investigate COVID-19's origin: it did not make the Huanan market environmental/metagenomic sequencing data public until 2023 (three years late), and briefly posted then restricted that data on GISAID in early 2023; it has not shared genetic sequences from ~508 viral samples from early Wuhan patients; and at least one team had early outpatient sequences deleted from a U.S. database. A senior WHO official called the withholding "inexcusable," and both the WHO and the FBI Director publicly accused China of obstructing the probe.
coverage_gaps: The contents of still-unreleased datasets and the reasons for each restriction are, by definition, unavailable.

nodes.label: Institutional opacity is treated as evidence about the concealed event rather than a general feature of the political environment.
policy_sensitivities: Weight changes with the assumed base rate of data restriction in unrelated Chinese crises and with whether process evidence is admitted into causal assessment.
correlation_risks: Highly overlaps database-offline, data-deletion, WHO-access, and RaTG13-disclosure items.

nodes.label: Deleted databases, withdrawn sequences, and restricted WHO access: behavior some read as a cover-up
nodes.claim: A cluster of data-access events is invoked as evidence of concealment: (1) the Wuhan Institute of Virology's (WIV) main online database of ~22,000 bat/virus sample records and sequences went offline on 12 September 2019, before the recognized outbreak; (2) a set of early Wuhan SARS-CoV-2 deep-sequencing datasets was deleted from the NIH Sequence Read Archive (SRA) at the submitters' request and later recovered by Jesse Bloom; and (3) China declined to provide raw line-list patient data on early cases to the WHO-convened joint mission and restricted aspects of its access. Proponents argue this is the pattern one would expect if there were something to hide.
coverage_gaps: Server logs, submitter correspondence, retained database contents, and complete access histories are missing.

nodes.label: Several heterogeneous access events are grouped as one pattern of suspicious conduct.
policy_sensitivities: The result depends on whether the policy aggregates events by apparent motive or evaluates each event's ordinary administrative base rate separately.
correlation_risks: Contains the same underlying events represented separately by database-offline and China-opacity items.

nodes.label: The 2018 DEFUSE proposal: a pre-pandemic blueprint to insert furin cleavage sites into bat SARS-related coronaviruses
nodes.claim: In early 2018 a consortium led by EcoHealth Alliance (Peter Daszak), with the University of North Carolina (Ralph Baric), the Wuhan Institute of Virology (Zhengli Shi), and Duke-NUS (Linfa Wang), submitted a grant proposal named DEFUSE to DARPA's PREEMPT program. The proposal described building full-length infectious clones of bat SARS-related coronaviruses and inserting "human-specific" proteolytic (furin) cleavage sites into spike proteins, with some experimental work to be performed at relatively low biosafety. DARPA rejected the proposal; the document was leaked publicly in September 2021.
coverage_gaps: No verified execution record, precursor sequence, laboratory notebook, or chain connecting DEFUSE work to SARS-CoV-2 is supplied.

nodes.label: A proposed experimental capability and conceptual match are treated as evidence that related work may have been performed.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on required match specificity: general cleavage-site work, S1/S2 furin insertion, or the exact SARS-CoV-2 sequence and backbone.
correlation_risks: One of three retained DEFUSE write-ups describing the same proposal; the cluster must count once.

nodes.label: The 2018 DEFUSE proposal planned to insert furin cleavage sites into SARS-like coronaviruses — a documented pre-pandemic 'blueprint'
nodes.claim: In a March 2018 grant proposal to DARPA ("Project DEFUSE"), a consortium led by EcoHealth Alliance, with the University of North Carolina (Ralph Baric) and the Wuhan Institute of Virology (Shi Zhengli), explicitly proposed introducing "human-specific cleavage sites" — i.e. proteolytic / furin-type cleavage sites — at the S1/S2 junction of bat SARS-related coronavirus spikes and assembling chimeric viruses from synthetic genome segments. SARS-CoV-2 subsequently appeared carrying a furin cleavage site at exactly that junction — a feature absent from all other known members of its sarbecovirus subgenus. The proposal was rejected by DARPA in 2018 and there is no documentary evidence the proposed experiment was ever performed.
coverage_gaps: Evidence of execution, the exact construct design, and a matching precursor are absent.

nodes.label: The coincidence between a proposed intervention and a later viral feature is treated as unlikely without a causal connection.
policy_sensitivities: Weight changes with priors for natural cleavage-site emergence and for unfunded or pilot work proceeding outside the grant.
correlation_risks: Duplicate DEFUSE evidence cluster; strongly correlated with the furin-site and laboratory-capability items.

nodes.label: The 2018 DEFUSE proposal proposed inserting furin cleavage sites into novel SARS-like coronaviruses in Wuhan
nodes.claim: A 2018 grant proposal ("DEFUSE") submitted by EcoHealth Alliance to DARPA, with the Wuhan Institute of Virology (WIV) and UNC's Ralph Baric as collaborators, explicitly proposed assembling chimeric bat SARS-like coronaviruses and inserting "human-specific" proteolytic cleavage sites — including a furin-cleavable site at the S1/S2 spike junction — and testing their growth in human airway cells. DARPA rejected the proposal and there is no documentary evidence the specific experiment was carried out.
coverage_gaps: No direct record shows the proposed experiment occurred or produced a SARS-CoV-2-like virus.

nodes.label: Documented intent and capability among named actors are treated as increasing the probability of undocumented execution.
policy_sensitivities: Assessment depends on whether primary proposal text, later advocacy interpretations, or molecular-specificity criteria dominate source selection.
correlation_risks: Third representation of the same DEFUSE proposal; duplicate prose broadens citations but not evidential independence.

nodes.label: Some of the earliest documented cases had no link to the Huanan market, and China gave the WHO only case summaries, not raw line-list data
nodes.claim: A substantial fraction of the earliest documented COVID-19 cases reported no exposure to the Huanan Seafood Market: in Huang et al. (Lancet, Jan 2020) only 27 of the first 41 hospitalized patients (66%) had direct market exposure, and the WHO–China Joint Study (2021) found roughly half of the 174 December-2019 Hubei cases were market-linked (one widely cited extraction is 55 of 168). Separately, a WHO team member (Dominic Dwyer) stated that China provided the WHO mission with case *summaries* rather than the raw "line-list" patient data the team requested.
coverage_gaps: Raw patient line lists, complete exposure histories, and independent early surveillance records are unavailable.

nodes.label: Lack of reported market exposure is treated as evidence that the market was not the origin rather than as expected incomplete linkage during community spread.
policy_sensitivities: Conclusion changes with case inclusion dates, direct-versus-geographic linkage rules, and confidence in official case summaries.
correlation_risks: Uses the same early-case dataset as market-clustering and absence-of-non-market-cluster items, sometimes to argue the opposite direction.

nodes.label: SARS-CoV-2-positive environmental samples concentrate at wildlife-selling stalls in the Huanan market
nodes.claim: SARS-CoV-2-positive environmental swabs collected at the Huanan Seafood Wholesale Market (from cages, carts, hair/feather-removal machines, freezers, floors, and drains) were spatially concentrated in the southwest corner of the market, the same section where live mammals were sold. The single stall with the most positive samples ("wildlife stall A") also contained abundant raccoon-dog and other susceptible-mammal genetic material in those same positive swabs.
coverage_gaps: No pre-closure animal samples, complete stall-level sampling frame, or direction-of-shedding evidence is available.

nodes.label: Spatial concentration of viral material at wildlife stalls is treated as evidence of infected animals rather than human shedding in a transmission site.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on whether environmental co-location without a positive animal is admitted as direct, indirect, or merely contextual evidence.
correlation_risks: Overlaps raccoon-dog co-location, live-mammal sales, and general market-spatial evidence.

nodes.label: FBI, DOE, and (in 2025) CIA lean toward a lab/research-related origin
nodes.claim: Three U.S. intelligence agencies lean toward a lab/research-related origin of COVID-19: the FBI (since at least 2021, publicly confirmed by Director Christopher Wray in Feb 2023) assesses with **moderate confidence** that the pandemic "most likely" arose from a lab incident in Wuhan; the Department of Energy shifted to a lab origin with **low confidence** in early 2023; and in **January 2025** the CIA assessed with **low confidence** that a research-related origin is more likely than a natural one. All three flag that both scenarios remain plausible, and the CIA's shift came from re-examining existing data, not new intelligence.
coverage_gaps: Underlying intelligence, agency likelihood models, dissent, and reasons for assessment changes are largely classified.

nodes.label: Agency expertise and classified access are treated as informative even when the underlying evidence cannot be inspected.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on deference policy, confidence-language interpretation, and whether shared intelligence inputs are treated as independent judgments.
correlation_risks: Agencies share information; this overlaps the ODNI split and House-report items and is not three independent evidence streams.

nodes.label: The furin cleavage site (PRRA insertion) is unique among known close sarbecovirus relatives
nodes.claim: SARS-CoV-2 carries a polybasic furin cleavage site (FCS) at the S1/S2 spike junction, encoded by a 12-nucleotide insertion that adds the amino acids PRRA (creating the cleavage motif RRAR↓), and this insertion is absent from every other known close relative within its sarbecovirus subgenus (RaTG13, BANAL-20-52, and others). The factual claim — that the FCS exists and is so far unique among sampled close sarbecoviruses — is essentially uncontested. The *inference* that this points to a lab origin is contested.
coverage_gaps: The natural sarbecovirus reservoir is sparsely sampled, and no complete experimental-record corpus is available.

nodes.label: Uniqueness among sampled close relatives is treated as evidence of artificial introduction rather than incomplete natural sampling.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on which coronavirus reference class is used and whether feature rarity is converted into a preregistered likelihood ratio.
correlation_risks: Central node shared by DEFUSE, CGG-codon, natural-recombination, and serial-passage arguments.

nodes.label: US House COVID Select final report (Dec 2024) concluded a lab-related incident is the most likely origin
nodes.claim: The Republican-led House Select Subcommittee on the Coronavirus Pandemic, in its December 2024 final report ("After Action Review of the COVID-19 Pandemic: Lessons Learned and a Path Forward," ~520 pages), concluded that SARS-CoV-2 "most likely" emerged from a laboratory- or research-related incident in Wuhan. Its case rests largely on a biological feature it calls "not found in nature" (the furin cleavage site), the single-introduction pattern, the absence of an identified intermediate host, reports of WIV researcher illnesses in fall 2019, and US-funded gain-of-function-adjacent work via EcoHealth Alliance/Peter Daszak.
coverage_gaps: The item does not identify a new public primary datum uniquely supporting the report's bottom-line conclusion.

nodes.label: A congressional committee conclusion is treated as epistemic evidence rather than a political synthesis of already-available evidence.
policy_sensitivities: Weight changes with policies for partisan institutions, expert testimony, and separation of primary evidence from institutional judgment.
correlation_risks: Repackages DEFUSE, biosafety, intelligence, and conduct evidence already represented elsewhere.

nodes.label: Earliest December 2019 cases cluster tightly around the Huanan market
nodes.claim: The residential locations of the earliest known December 2019 COVID-19 cases in Wuhan — including those with no reported epidemiological link to the Huanan Seafood Wholesale Market — were geographically centered on that market, with the spatial pattern dispersing across the city by January–February 2020. Worobey et al. argue this centering would be extremely unlikely unless the outbreak began at or very near the market.
coverage_gaps: Independent raw line-list data, original geocoding, and complete case-finding procedures are unavailable.

nodes.label: The reconstructed early-case locations are representative enough for spatial centrality to identify an outbreak epicenter.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on null model, case-set definition, ascertainment-bias treatment, and distinction between epicenter and spillover site.
correlation_risks: Same market/case dataset underlies several natural-origin evidence items and should be clustered.

nodes.label: Huanan market spatial clustering and two zoonotic introductions
nodes.claim: Two peer-reviewed Science papers (Worobey et al. 2022; Pekar et al. 2022), plus a follow-up Cell paper (Crits-Christoph et al. 2024), report that (a) the earliest December 2019 COVID-19 cases were geographically centered on the Huanan Seafood Wholesale Market, (b) SARS-CoV-2-positive environmental samples within the market were spatially associated with stalls selling live mammals, with raccoon-dog genetic material co-located with viral RNA, and (c) molecular-clock and phylodynamic analysis supports at least two separate introductions into humans (lineages A and B), a pattern the authors argue fits repeated live-wildlife-trade spillover.
coverage_gaps: Raw early-case data, complete early sequences, and direct animal infections are absent.

nodes.label: Spatial clustering and two early lineages are combined as mutually reinforcing evidence of repeated market spillover.
policy_sensitivities: Result changes with spatial ascertainment assumptions and with the phylogenetic model used to infer one versus multiple introductions.
correlation_risks: Composite of two separately repeated evidence clusters; combining them can hide dependence and double counting.

nodes.label: Live SARS-CoV-2-susceptible mammals were documented on sale in Wuhan markets, including Huanan, before the pandemic
nodes.claim: Systematic monthly surveys of Wuhan wet markets from May 2017 to November 2019 documented 47,381 live wild animals from 38 species (18 of them mammals) on sale in poor hygienic and welfare conditions, including raccoon dogs, masked palm civets, and American mink — species known to be susceptible to SARS-like coronaviruses. Seven shops at the Huanan Seafood Market were among those selling live mammals. No bats or pangolins were observed in the trade.
coverage_gaps: Animals present during the relevant period were removed before virological sampling; supply-chain infection data are incomplete.

nodes.label: Presence of susceptible animals creates a mechanism-bearing prior for market spillover even without an identified infected animal.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on whether opportunity evidence is evaluated symmetrically for wildlife-trade and laboratory scenarios.
correlation_risks: Overlaps environmental-stall and raccoon-dog genetic evidence; all bear on the same market-animal mechanism.

nodes.label: 2015 SHC014 chimeric SARS-like coronavirus (Menachery, Baric, Shi): documented gain-of-function capability at UNC/WIV
nodes.claim: In 2015, a Nature Medicine paper co-authored by the Wuhan Institute of Virology's Shi Zhengli (with UNC's Ralph Baric and lead author Vineet Menachery) reported building a lab chimera that placed the spike protein of a WIV-collected bat coronavirus (SHC014) onto a mouse-adapted SARS-CoV backbone, and showed the resulting virus replicated efficiently in primary human airway epithelial cells. The paper itself flagged that the work touched on the then-active U.S. gain-of-function (GoF) research funding pause.
coverage_gaps: No specific construct, precursor, experiment, or chain connects SHC014 work to SARS-CoV-2.

nodes.label: Demonstrated capability to construct a SARS-like chimera is treated as evidence relevant to a later event involving different actors, viruses, and records.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on whether general capability evidence is admitted and how collaboration links are translated into event probability.
correlation_risks: Correlated with DEFUSE, WIV biosafety, and general gain-of-function capability arguments.

nodes.label: The Mojiang mine, the 2012 miner deaths, and RaTG13's contested provenance
nodes.claim: RaTG13 — the bat coronavirus ~96.1% identical to SARS-CoV-2 — was sequenced by the Wuhan Institute of Virology (WIV) from a bat-swab sample originally published under the partial-sequence label BtCoV/4991, collected in 2013 from the Tongguan mineshaft in Mojiang, Yunnan, where in 2012 six miners clearing bat guano fell ill with severe pneumonia and three died. WIV did not initially disclose, in its February 2020 *Nature* paper describing RaTG13, that RaTG13 and BtCoV/4991 were the same sample, nor the mine's fatal-illness history; both were disclosed/confirmed only later (Rahalkar & Bahulikar; the November 2020 *Nature* addendum).
coverage_gaps: Complete miner clinical samples, WIV sample inventory, sequencing history, and database records are unavailable.

nodes.label: Ambiguity around miner illness and sample naming is treated as relevant to whether WIV possessed a closer precursor.
policy_sensitivities: Weight changes with policies for contested theses, later addenda, advocacy reconstructions, and institutional disclosure conduct.
correlation_risks: Shares underlying mine, RaTG13, and disclosure events with the RaTG13-secrecy item.

nodes.label: Molecular-clock dating places SARS-CoV-2's common ancestor in Nov–Dec 2019, with no genomic sign of long cryptic human circulation
nodes.claim: Bayesian molecular-clock phylodynamics place the time of the most recent common ancestor (tMRCA) of sequenced SARS-CoV-2 genomes in roughly mid-November to mid-December 2019, with the inferred index case / first human infections in the weeks just before (plausibly mid-October to mid-November 2019). The sampled genomic diversity is shallow and shows no robust signal of a long, deep tail of cryptic human transmission predating the Wuhan outbreak — a pattern consistent with a recent introduction near the outbreak epicenter.
coverage_gaps: The earliest infections and potentially informative unsampled or withheld sequences are absent.

nodes.label: The estimated common-ancestor date constrains competing emergence narratives and the duration of unobserved circulation.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on sequence inclusion, clock model, recombination assumptions, and treatment of uncertain early genomes.
correlation_risks: Correlated with early-case timing and lineage-introduction analyses that use overlapping sequence data.

nodes.label: Pervasive natural recombination in sarbecoviruses, and a SARS-CoV-2 lineage that diverged decades before 2019
nodes.claim: Recombination-aware phylogenetics shows that bat sarbecoviruses recombine extensively in nature (Lytras et al. detected ~160 recombination events across 78 genomes), and that the SARS-CoV-2 lineage diverged from its closest sampled relatives (RaTG13, RmYN02, pangolin and Southeast-Asian viruses) decades to over a century ago. The receptor-binding domain (RBD) features that allow human-ACE2 binding appear to be inherited from a deep common ancestor rather than recently acquired, and SARS-CoV-2 is not a recombinant of any virus sampled to date.
coverage_gaps: No sampled recombination chain reconstructs the complete SARS-CoV-2 progenitor.

nodes.label: Demonstrating a natural mechanism for mosaic genomes reduces the need to posit engineering for SARS-CoV-2's feature combination.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on whether mechanism plausibility or identification of the actual ancestral pathway is required.
correlation_risks: Mechanistically overlaps BANAL, pangolin-RBD, genetic-distance, and furin-site natural-origin arguments.

nodes.label: No close progenitor virus or infected intermediate host has been found years after emergence
nodes.claim: More than four years after the emergence of SARS-CoV-2, no direct animal progenitor virus has been sampled and no infected non-human animal of any species (intermediate host or reservoir) has been found that links to the pandemic, despite substantial sampling. The closest known relatives are bat viruses (~96–96.8% identity) whose shared ancestor with SARS-CoV-2 dates back several decades.
coverage_gaps: The relevant wildlife reservoir, farms, supply chains, and laboratory inventories were not comprehensively sampled.

nodes.label: Failure to find a close progenitor or host is treated as less expected under zoonosis than under a laboratory incident.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on search-effort estimates, reservoir size, political access, and expected discovery time under each hypothesis.
correlation_risks: Overlaps no-source-animal and RaTG13/BANAL genetic-distance items.

nodes.label: No infected source animal was ever found at the Huanan market or in its supply chain
nodes.claim: China CDC sampling of 457 animal specimens spanning ~18 species at the Huanan Seafood Market detected SARS-CoV-2 in environmental swabs but in zero animal samples, and subsequent tracing of wildlife farms and supply chains has not identified any infected progenitor or intermediate host animal. Lab-leak proponents cite this persistent absence as a notable evidentiary gap relative to SARS-CoV-1 (civets) and MERS (camels), where infected intermediate hosts were found relatively quickly.
coverage_gaps: No contemporaneous animal testing occurred at the market, and upstream farms and suppliers were incompletely investigated.

nodes.label: A zoonotic market origin should have produced a detectable infected animal despite the delay and removal of live animals.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on the counterfactual probability of detection given actual sampling timing and supply-chain disruption.
correlation_risks: Narrower version of the no-close-progenitor/intermediate-host evidence and correlated with market-animal items.

nodes.label: US Intelligence Community is split on COVID-19 origins, with no agency above moderate confidence
nodes.claim: The declassified ODNI assessments (August 2021, updated June 2023) show a genuinely divided US Intelligence Community: the FBI favors a laboratory-associated origin with *moderate* confidence and the Department of Energy with *low* confidence, while four other IC elements plus the National Intelligence Council favor natural exposure with *low* confidence, and at least the CIA remained unable to choose. The IC consensus is that the virus was not developed as a biological weapon and (with low confidence) was probably not genetically engineered. No agency reaches better than "moderate" confidence on either side.
coverage_gaps: Underlying reports, sources, interagency dependencies, and analytic methods are classified.

nodes.label: A split among intelligence agencies is treated as evidence that both hypotheses remain plausible and that public evidence is incomplete.
policy_sensitivities: Interpretation depends on deference to classified expertise and whether agency counts or confidence-weighted reasoning are used.
correlation_risks: Same institutional evidence family as the FBI/DOE/CIA and House-report items.

nodes.label: Guangdong pangolin coronaviruses share the key ACE2-contact RBD residues with SARS-CoV-2
nodes.claim: Coronaviruses recovered from smuggled Malayan pangolins seized in Guangdong province (notably the lineage often called Pangolin-CoV / GD/MP789) have a receptor-binding domain (RBD) ~97% identical to SARS-CoV-2's, and match SARS-CoV-2 at the (five of six) critical RBD residues that contact human ACE2, whereas the bat virus RaTG13 — closest to SARS-CoV-2 across the rest of the genome — matches at only one. The human-binding interface therefore has a clear natural analog in viruses sampled from wild animals.
coverage_gaps: Pangolin viruses are not close whole-genome progenitors, and the precise recombination pathway is unsampled.

nodes.label: Natural occurrence of similar RBD residues in pangolin coronaviruses demonstrates a plausible natural source for that genomic component.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on whether mosaic feature plausibility or a complete direct progenitor is required.
correlation_risks: Correlated with natural-recombination and BANAL ACE2-binding evidence.

nodes.label: 'Proximal Origin' authors privately called a lab leak plausible while their paper publicly ruled it out
nodes.claim: The Andersen et al. "Proximal Origin of SARS-CoV-2" paper (Nature Medicine, March 2020) concluded that "we do not believe that any type of laboratory-based scenario is plausible," yet contemporaneous Slack messages and emails — released in 2023 — show the same authors privately describing accidental lab escape as "highly likely" and "not some fringe theory" in the very weeks they drafted it, following a 1 February 2020 call convened by Fauci, Collins, and Farrar.
coverage_gaps: Complete message context, evolving analyses, interviews, and contemporaneous evidence available to participants are not fully represented.

nodes.label: Private uncertainty, rapid opinion change, or institutional coordination is treated as evidence about the reliability or independence of the published conclusion.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on whether discourse/process evidence is admitted and how preliminary private deliberation is distinguished from suppression.
correlation_risks: Overlaps broader conduct, opacity, and institutional-trust evidence rather than providing new biological evidence.

nodes.label: Genetic tracing co-locates susceptible wildlife (raccoon dogs) with SARS-CoV-2 in the most positive market stall
nodes.claim: Reanalysis of the China CDC environmental metatranscriptomic dataset shows that SARS-CoV-2-positive samples from the Huanan Seafood Market contained mitochondrial genetic material from SARS-CoV-2-susceptible mammals — notably raccoon dogs (*Nyctereutes procyonoides*), plus civets, bamboo rats, hedgehogs and porcupines. The samples cluster at and around one wildlife stall ("wildlife stall A") in the market's southwest corner, where live mammals had been sold, and that stall had the highest SARS-CoV-2 positivity rate.
coverage_gaps: No intact infected animal, animal serology, or temporal sample establishes direction of transmission.

nodes.label: Co-location of susceptible-animal DNA and viral RNA is treated as evidence that animals, rather than nearby humans, shed the virus.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on standards for metagenomic co-occurrence, contamination controls, and whether indirect animal evidence is admitted.
correlation_risks: Same market environmental samples support the wildlife-stall positivity item; do not count separately without decomposing claims.

nodes.label: RaTG13 and BANAL-52 are close relatives but represent decades of divergence from SARS-CoV-2
nodes.claim: The two closest known relatives of SARS-CoV-2, the bat coronaviruses RaTG13 (~96.1% genome identity) and BANAL-52 (~96.8% identity), each differ from SARS-CoV-2 by roughly 900–1,100 nucleotides across the ~30 kb genome, corresponding to several decades of estimated evolutionary divergence; neither, nor any other known sarbecovirus, carries the furin (S1/S2 polybasic) cleavage site found in SARS-CoV-2.
coverage_gaps: Unknown wildlife viruses and unreleased laboratory sequences prevent a complete nearest-neighbor assessment.

nodes.label: Large divergence from known relatives is used to constrain both direct natural and known-laboratory precursor narratives.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on molecular-clock assumptions and the expected unsampled diversity in wildlife and laboratory collections.
correlation_risks: Shares source and mechanism base with BANAL, natural-recombination, and no-close-progenitor items.

nodes.label: RaTG13's Mojiang mine provenance and the WIV's delayed, incomplete disclosure
nodes.claim: RaTG13 — the bat coronavirus that in February 2020 was SARS-CoV-2's closest known relative (~96.1–96.2% genome identity) — was collected by the Wuhan Institute of Virology (WIV) from an abandoned mineshaft near Tongguan, Mojiang County, Yunnan, the same mine where six miners cleaning bat guano in 2012 developed severe pneumonia and three died. The WIV's February 2020 Nature paper (Zhou et al.) did not disclose that RaTG13 was the same sample previously published as BtCoV/4991, nor that it came from this mine, nor the miners' illness; these links were acknowledged only belatedly (notably in a November 2020 Nature addendum) after outside investigators reconstructed them.
coverage_gaps: Complete sample inventories, sequence history, mine records, and internal WIV communications are unavailable.

nodes.label: Delayed disclosure and renaming are treated as possible evidence of concealment of a more relevant laboratory lineage.
policy_sensitivities: Weight changes with norms for virology naming, publication disclosure, and institutional transparency.
correlation_risks: Same Mojiang/RaTG13 event as the neutral provenance item and correlated with database and opacity evidence.

nodes.label: SARS-CoV-2's receptor-binding domain was not computationally optimal for human ACE2 at emergence
nodes.claim: At the time SARS-CoV-2 emerged, its receptor-binding domain (RBD) bound human ACE2 efficiently but by a structural solution **not predicted to be optimal** by the computational/structural models then available. Andersen et al. (2020) argued this is more consistent with natural selection than with deliberate engineering, because a designer using those models would have chosen a different, "ideal" binding configuration. Subsequent variants that *improved* ACE2 affinity show the original RBD was not at a fitness peak.
coverage_gaps: The item does not test all plausible engineering or passage workflows.

nodes.label: A rational designer would have used then-current computational predictions and selected an apparently optimal ACE2-binding solution.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on whether the competing laboratory hypothesis specifies rational design, directed evolution, serial passage, or collection of a natural virus.
correlation_risks: Correlated with BANAL natural ACE2 binding and broader RBD-naturalness arguments.

nodes.label: SARS-CoV-1 (2002–04) emerged through the live wildlife trade at Chinese markets — a close precedent for a market-origin zoonosis
nodes.claim: SARS-CoV-1, the cause of the 2002–04 SARS epidemic, crossed into humans through the live wildlife trade: a SARS-related coronavirus was isolated from masked palm civets (and detected in a raccoon dog) in a Guangdong live-animal market, market animal traders showed markedly elevated SARS-CoV antibody prevalence, and the 2003–04 second outbreak was traced to civet exposure and ended after markets were closed and civets culled. This is a documented, closely analogous precedent for a market/wildlife-trade origin of a SARS-related coronavirus in China.
coverage_gaps: The analogy does not supply event-specific evidence about SARS-CoV-2.

nodes.label: SARS-CoV-1 is selected as a close reference class for setting prior odds on SARS-CoV-2 emergence.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on reference-class choice and how differences in host discovery, surveillance, geography, and laboratory context are handled.
correlation_risks: Overlaps the combined SARS-1/MERS zoonotic-precedent item.

nodes.label: Documented zoonotic precedent: SARS-CoV-1 and MERS-CoV both arose naturally via intermediate hosts
nodes.claim: The two prior deadly human coronaviruses of the 21st century — SARS-CoV-1 (2002–2003) and MERS-CoV (2012–present) — were each established as natural zoonotic spillovers through animal intermediate hosts: market-traded masked palm civets and raccoon dogs in Guangdong, China for SARS-CoV-1, and dromedary camels in the Arabian Peninsula (and Africa) for MERS-CoV. In neither case is there evidence of laboratory involvement, and archived serology shows the MERS-CoV intermediate host was infected years before the first recognized human case.
coverage_gaps: A sample of two confirmed precedents cannot establish a stable base rate and may be selected partly because those cases were resolved.

nodes.label: The two recognized deadly coronavirus emergences form an appropriate and sufficiently complete reference class.
policy_sensitivities: Prior odds vary with whether the reference class includes all zoonoses, all laboratory accidents, unresolved outbreaks, or only close coronaviruses.
correlation_risks: Contains SARS-CoV-1 precedent again and should not be added independently to the dedicated SARS-1 item.

nodes.label: The serial-passage / cell-culture adaptation hypothesis — and why the intact furin site in early isolates argues against it
nodes.claim: A lab-origin variant holds that a natural bat sarbecovirus, repeatedly grown ("serially passaged") in cell culture or in humanized/animal models, could have acquired human-adapted features — including, on some versions, the furin cleavage site (FCS) — and then leaked, leaving **no engineering signature** in the genome (the "no-see-um" scenario). This document assesses that specific mechanistic claim, not the broader lab-leak question.
coverage_gaps: No precursor, passage series, experimental record, or matching model-system result is supplied.

nodes.label: A mechanistically possible, signature-free passage pathway receives probability even without positive evidence that it occurred.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on whether possibility, demonstrated precedent, or event-specific records are required for inclusion.
correlation_risks: Correlated with furin-site, laboratory-capability, WIV-biosafety, and missing-record arguments.

nodes.label: Reports that three WIV researchers fell ill in November 2019
nodes.claim: A U.S. State Department fact sheet (15 January 2021) stated that "several researchers inside the WIV became sick in autumn 2019, before the first identified case of the outbreak, with symptoms consistent with both COVID-19 and common seasonal illnesses." Later reporting (Wall Street Journal and NBC News, May 2021), citing an undisclosed U.S. intelligence report, said that *three* specific WIV researchers fell ill enough to seek hospital care in November 2019. The factual core in dispute is twofold: (a) whether the researchers were genuinely sick at that time, and (b) whether their illness was COVID-19 as opposed to ordinary seasonal sickness. The first part is moderately supported; the second is essentially unestablished.
coverage_gaps: Medical records, contemporaneous tests, serology, precise dates, and independently attributable identities are unavailable.

nodes.label: A small cluster of autumn respiratory illness among laboratory staff is treated as anomalous relative to the seasonal baseline.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on admission of anonymous intelligence reporting and on the base rate assigned to ordinary illness and hospital visits.
correlation_risks: News reports largely trace to overlapping intelligence claims rather than independent witnesses.

nodes.label: Two distinct early lineages (A and B) and the inference of multiple market spillovers
nodes.claim: Before February 2020, SARS-CoV-2 genomic diversity comprised two distinct lineages, A and B, separated by exactly two nucleotide substitutions (C8782T and T28144C), with no intermediate haplotypes that survive scrutiny. Pekar et al. (2022) argue this pattern is best explained by at least two independent zoonotic introductions into humans around mid-to-late November 2019, and that both lineages have now been detected in samples tied to the Huanan Seafood Market. The two-introduction inference is the contested part; the descriptive genomic facts are not.
coverage_gaps: Early sequence sampling is incomplete, and disputed intermediate genomes and model implementations require independent reproduction.

nodes.label: Two early lineages and the absence of accepted intermediates are modeled as multiple introductions rather than rapid divergence after one introduction.
policy_sensitivities: Conclusion depends on topology constraints, inclusion of disputed genomes, model symmetry, and error corrections.
correlation_risks: Duplicate of the two-zoonotic-introductions item and overlaps market-linked sequence evidence.

nodes.label: Two early lineages (A and B) and the inference of two separate zoonotic spillovers at the Huanan market
nodes.claim: Early SARS-CoV-2 diversity comprised two distinct lineages, A and B, differing by exactly two nucleotides (positions 8782 and 28144). Pekar et al. (2022) argue from phylodynamic simulation that these two lineages were introduced into humans by at least two *separate* spillover events around mid-to-late November 2019, both geographically and temporally linked to the Huanan Seafood Wholesale Market — the pattern expected from repeated animal-to-human transmission at a live-wildlife market rather than from a single point-source release.
coverage_gaps: Full independent reproduction of the code, model constraints, and disputed early genomes is not part of the baseline.

nodes.label: The two-lineage pattern is treated as more expected from repeated animal exposures than from one introduction followed by early divergence.
policy_sensitivities: Weight changes with corrected Bayes factors, hypothesis symmetry, peer-review thresholds, and treatment of later critiques.
correlation_risks: Same underlying claim as the other lineage-A/B item and part of the market evidence cluster.

nodes.label: WHO-China 2021 'extremely unlikely' finding was repudiated by the WHO's own leadership and later reopened
nodes.claim: The March 2021 WHO-convened joint study with China ranked introduction via a laboratory incident as "extremely unlikely," the lowest of four hypotheses. Within four months, WHO Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus publicly called the push to rule out a lab leak "premature," said the joint team had been denied access to raw data, and (in August 2021) said Chinese scientists had pressured the team to drop the lab hypothesis. The 2022 SAGO preliminary report then re-opened the laboratory hypothesis as warranting further investigation, and the 2025 SAGO final report concluded the investigation remained incomplete because China still had not shared key data.
coverage_gaps: Key data requested by WHO/SAGO remain unavailable, preventing resolution of why confidence should move.

nodes.label: WHO's change in public language is treated as evidence about the underlying origin rather than primarily about process and evidential sufficiency.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on deference to institutional conclusions and separation of procedural criticism from substantive probability updates.
correlation_risks: Overlaps China-data-withholding and general institutional-process evidence.

nodes.label: WIV handled novel SARS-related bat coronaviruses at BSL-2, and Shi Zhengli's request for higher containment was reportedly denied
nodes.claim: Documents and statements indicate that the Wuhan Institute of Virology (WIV) cultured and manipulated some novel SARS-related bat coronaviruses at biosafety level 2 (BSL-2) and BSL-3 rather than the highest containment (BSL-4). Separately, a 2018 U.S. State Department cable reported that Shi Zhengli's request to study SARS-related work in the WIV's new BSL-4 facility was denied by China's National Health and Family Planning Commission, with no reason given. The DEFUSE proposal (2018) further shows the WIV/EcoHealth team planned novel-coronavirus engineering work at BSL-2.
coverage_gaps: Facility-specific procedures, incident logs, staff exposure records, and evidence that WIV possessed a direct precursor are absent.

nodes.label: Permitted BSL-2/3 work with novel bat coronaviruses is treated as a materially elevated route to the specific pandemic virus.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on contemporaneous biosafety norms, virus pathogenicity known at the time, and whether opportunity evidence is admitted.
correlation_risks: Overlaps State Department cables, Menachery/Baric capability, DEFUSE, and general lab-opportunity evidence.

nodes.label: WIV bat coronavirus sequence database taken offline in September 2019
nodes.claim: The Wuhan Institute of Virology's (WIV) main bat- and rodent-virus sample/sequence database (hosted at `csdata.org` / `batvirus.whiov.ac.cn`), which held tens of thousands of sample records and a body of unpublished bat coronavirus sequences, became inaccessible from outside the institute around September 12, 2019 — roughly three months before the COVID-19 outbreak was publicly acknowledged. The full underlying data has never been released to independent investigators.
coverage_gaps: Server logs, maintenance records, internal access, complete database contents, and contemporaneous reasons are unavailable.

nodes.label: Loss of public access shortly before recognized emergence is treated as intentional concealment related to the outbreak.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on base rates for research-site outages, exact accessibility timelines, and whether later non-release is bundled with the initial outage.
correlation_risks: One event already included in the broader data-deletion and China-opacity clusters.

nodes.label: Pandemic emerged in Wuhan, home to the world's leading bat-SARS-coronavirus lab, ~1,500-1,800 km from the nearest known viral reservoir
nodes.claim: A novel SARS-related coronavirus first erupted in Wuhan, the city hosting the Wuhan Institute of Virology (WIV) — the world's leading collector and laboratory manipulator of SARS-like bat coronaviruses — even though the closest known wild relatives circulate in horseshoe bats in Yunnan (China) and Laos, roughly 1,500-1,800 km away from Wuhan. Lab-leak proponents argue this geographic juxtaposition is an extraordinary coincidence.
coverage_gaps: No quantified comparison estimates the probability of Wuhan under laboratory and natural-trade pathways using symmetric geographic models.

nodes.label: Wuhan is selected after the event as a city unusually associated with bat-coronavirus research, making the co-location appear diagnostically rare.
policy_sensitivities: Weight depends on the reference class of cities, labs, outbreak-detection hubs, wildlife trade routes, and whether location hypotheses were specified in advance.
correlation_risks: Correlated with WIV capability and biosafety evidence, while competing market geography is represented by several overlapping items.
